---
title: "Stage 4: CLIP and Vision-Language Models"
date: today
---

## Reading

Radford et al. 2021, *Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision*.
[https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.00020](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.00020)

As a gentler entry point before the paper, watch:
**"CLIP explained" by Yannic Kilcher** -- search on YouTube, roughly 30 minutes.

Estimated time: 4-5 hours.

## Key concepts to watch for

- Contrastive learning: what the training objective is and why it works
- Shared embedding space: what it means for images and text to live in the same space
- InfoNCE loss: the specific contrastive loss CLIP uses and its information-theoretic interpretation
- Zero-shot classification: how CLIP classifies images it has never explicitly been trained on
- Training data: what LAION and web-scraped image-text pairs look like, and why this matters
- Cosine similarity: how "closeness" is measured in the embedding space

## Checkpoint

After finishing the reading, answer these in your own words:

1. What is contrastive learning? What is the model being trained to do, 
and what does the loss function reward and penalise?
2. What does it mean for images and text to share an embedding space? 
What is geometrically happening when a photo of a dog and the caption 
"a photo of a dog" end up close together?
3. How does zero-shot classification work in CLIP? Walk through the 
mechanism step by step.
4. CLIP is trained on web-scraped image-text pairs. What are the 
implications of this for what the embedding space learns?
5. Why is cosine similarity used to measure closeness rather than 
Euclidean distance?

*(answers below)*

## My answers

*(to be filled after reading)*

## Open questions

## Notes
